coredata.dc:title: Gadolinium (III) doped carbon dots as dual-mode sensor for the recognition of dopamine hydrochloride and glutamate enantiomers with logic gate operation

coredata.dc:description: Dopamine hydrochloride (DH) and D-Glutamic acid (D-Glu) are important excitatory neurotransmitters, which are closely relative to central nervous system diseases. Therefore, it is critical to develop the sensitive and facile sensor to precisely monitor the changes of these neurotransmitters. Herein, the gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) were synthesized by a low-cost and effortless one-pot solvothermal method. These CDs exhibited rapid and reliable fluorescent and colorimetric response signals towards DH and D-Glu. Interestingly, the fluorescence of Gd-CDs could be selectively quenched by DH owing to the fact that the Gd-CDs could coordinate with phenolic hydroxyl groups of DH. Moreover, the quench process was effectively inhibited because the D-Glu competitively coordinated with Gd-CDs-DH system to form a more stable complex. In fluorescence mode, the designed fluorescence sensor possessed an excellent linear relationship for DH in the range from 1 to 10 μM with a low detection limit of 1.26 nM, and the fluorescence could be selectively recovered by D-Glu. In colorimetric manner, DH and D-Glu could be detected by UV–Vis absorption spectrum in the range of 1–15 μM and 1–1.50 mM, respectively. Moreover, the proposed method could not only easily monitor the DH and D-Glu in aqueous solutions as well as mouse serum and human urine samples, but also be employed for detecting DH and D-Glu in cells. Fortunately, the fluorescent and colorimetric dual readout AND logic operation was successfully demonstrated in all-aqueous media. Accordingly, the prepared Gd-CDs hold the potential to become a promising nano-sensor for DH and D-Glu sensing in disease diagnosis areas.

head.ce:title: Gadolinium (III) doped carbon dots as dual-mode sensor for the recognition of dopamine hydrochloride and glutamate enantiomers with logic gate operation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine hydrochloride (DH) and D-Glutamic acid (D-Glu) are important excitatory neurotransmitters, which are closely relative to central nervous system diseases. Therefore, it is critical to develop the sensitive and facile sensor to precisely monitor the changes of these neurotransmitters. Herein, the gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) were synthesized by a low-cost and effortless one-pot solvothermal method. These CDs exhibited rapid and reliable fluorescent and colorimetric response signals towards DH and D-Glu. Interestingly, the fluorescence of Gd-CDs could be selectively quenched by DH owing to the fact that the Gd-CDs could coordinate with phenolic hydroxyl groups of DH. Moreover, the quench process was effectively inhibited because the D-Glu competitively coordinated with Gd-CDs-DH system to form a more stable complex. In fluorescence mode, the designed fluorescence sensor possessed an excellent linear relationship for DH in the range from 1 to 10 μM with a low detection limit of 1.26 nM, and the fluorescence could be selectively recovered by D-Glu. In colorimetric manner, DH and D-Glu could be detected by UV–Vis absorption spectrum in the range of 1–15 μM and 1–1.50 mM, respectively. Moreover, the proposed method could not only easily monitor the DH and D-Glu in aqueous solutions as well as mouse serum and human urine samples, but also be employed for detecting DH and D-Glu in cells. Fortunately, the fluorescent and colorimetric dual readout AND logic operation was successfully demonstrated in all-aqueous media. Accordingly, the prepared Gd-CDs hold the potential to become a promising nano-sensor for DH and D-Glu sensing in disease diagnosis areas.

Scheme 1 Gd-CDs as fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensor for DH and D-Glu sensing and multi-type cell imaging. As a consequence, these distinguished characteristics could endow this new sensor with a broad application in biosensing in the future.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), as a monoamine neurotransmitter, plays a vital role in the human central nervous, kidney, cardiovascular, and hormonal system [1,2]. The DA metabolism deregulation may cause a range of neurological diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease, Epilepsy and genetic disorders including Menkes and Wilson's diseases [3]. Particularly, the concentration of DA in the blood of Parkinson's disease patients is lower than 10−10 M, which makes the DA detection face major challenge for the medical community [4]. Moreover, the DA is extremely unstable and easily oxidized to dopamine quinone in the air [5]. Therefore, the dopamine hydrochloride (DH) is general to replace DA in actual testing [6]. Regrettably, many quantitative questions about DH in vivo and in vitro have not been well answered. Glutamic acid (Glu) is a significant neurotransmitter (NT) in the mammalian brain [7]. It commonly serves as a signiﬁcant biomarker for early diagnosis of liver disease for the humans [8,9]. L-Glutamic acid (L-Glu) is one of the most important excitatory signaling molecules in the central nervous system, which plays the dominant role in the formation and stability of brain functions [10,11]. However, recent studies have shown that D-Glutamic acid (D-Glu) is not only a crucial component of bacterial glycan, but also the pathogen of anthracnose [12,13]. Furthermore, D-amino acid is commonly man-made, which can't be metabolized by living systems and may even adversely affect life [14]. Thus, the presence of D-amino acid in food and medicine can lead to poor nutrition and low safety of products [15,16]. Therefore, establishing an effective analytical method to monitor the changes of D-Glu concentration has important research significance in the early diagnosis of clinical medical diseases and food safety [17].

Till now, various DH sensing techniques have been established including high performance liquid chromatography [18], chromatography-mass spectrometry [19], and flow injection techniques [20]. Moreover, a number of approaches also have been developed for monitoring Glu enantiomers such as spectrophotometry [21], capillary electrophoresis [22] and electrochemistry [10]. Nevertheless, these conventional methods have the drawbacks such as the requirement for chiral selection agent, low economic benefit, long time of analysis and lack of portable [14,15]. To achieve highly selective sensing DH and recognition chiral amino acids, the fluorescence sensing manner is considered to be a promising approach due to low-damage, high sensitivity, fast response, simple operation as well as real-time monitoring [4,23]. Meanwhile, the colorimetric methods have also attracted the unique attention of researchers in recent years because of its visualization, convenience and speediness [24]. Thus, the combination of fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode methods can not only provide high sensitivity through fluorescence signals, but also achieve naked eye visualization of target analytes through UV–Vis absorption, and make detection results more reliable [25]. Therefore, developing the fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensors with superiority in biocompatibility, convenience, reliability and accuracy for detecting DH and D-Glu in water solution and living cells is still a big problem to be solved [10,26–28].

Carbon nanomaterials including carbon dots (CDs), single-walled carbon nanotubes, and graphite nanoplatelets, have attracted considerable attention in recent years [29]. Among them, CDs have been successfully employed to various biological fields of biological imaging, drug or gene delivery, antimicrobial and biological sensing own to their remarkable advantages such as abundant low-cost sources, three-dimensional nanostructure, unique photostability, excellent water solubility, easily functionalities and high biological safety [14,30,31]. Especially in the direction of biosensing, plenty of researchers have made great efforts based on CDs for the detection of various targets including metal ions, small biological molecules and reactive oxygen species [26]. However, bifunctional sensors are rare to be reported, which can achieve the enantioselective recognition and quantification of amino acids [32]. For example, Zheng and her colleagues successfully synthesized orange-red emitting CDs as a fluorescent/colorimetric dual-signal probe to sensitively detect Ag+ and selectively recognize L-cysteine (L-Cys) [24]. Ren and his colleagues prepared dual-mode CDs as reversible fluorescent/colorimetric probe for dynamic detecting ClO− and L-ascorbic acid (L-AA) in cells [25]. Zhang and his colleagues obtained phenylenediamine-based red-emitting N-CDs as colorimetric/ﬂuorescent probe for intuitive and accurate detection of L-glutathione (L-GSH) [33]. Hence, to design a kind of simple and accurate fluorescent/colorimetric sensor for biological small molecules and the distinction of amino acid enantiomers face highly challenge [15].

In this work, the dual pattern recognition sensor of Gd-CDs was developed by one-pot solvothermal route. As shown in Scheme 1 , the fluorescent and colorimetric signals of the obtained Gd-CDs could effectively respond to DH and D-Glu and are suitable for multi-type cell imaging. As expected, these prepared CDs could serve as a sensitive fluorescent and colorimetric sensor for DH and D-Glu detection in actual samples. Intriguingly, the prepared Gd-CDs as FL/Abs dual readout logic gate operation could be successfully read out in water medium. As a consequence, these distinguished characteristics could endow this new sensor with a broad application in biosensing in the future.

Polyetherimide (PEI, MW = 40 KDa), L/D-arginine (L/D-Arg), L/D-histidine (L/D-His), glycine (Gly), valine (Val), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and gadolinium chloride (GdCl3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co., Ltd (China). Dopamine hydrochloride (DH) and NaCl were supplied by Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd (China). L/D-phenylalanine (L/D-Phe) and L/D-lisoleucine (L/D-Lle) were obtained from Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (Japan). L/D-aspartic acid (L/D-Asp), L/D-glutamic acid (L/D-Glu), L/D-lysine (L/D-Lys), L/D-tryptophan (L/D-Try), L/D-proline (L/D-Pro) and L/D-cysteine (L/D-Cys) were provided by Beijing Ding Chang Sheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (China). LiCl, MgCl2, AlCl3, PbCl2, ZnCl2, CaCl2, and glucose (Glu) were bought from Tianjing Tianli Reagent Co., Ltd (China). 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM) were purchased from Biological Industries (Israel). Ultra-pure water was produced by MING CHE Water Purification System equipment (China).

The rare earth element doped CDs were prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal method. Initially, GdCl3 (0.02 g) and PEI (2 mL) were dissolved in DMF (13 mL) solution. Then, the mixed solution was stirred and transferred into a 50 mL Teflon lined autoclave with heating at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the bright yellow solution was added to a dialysis bag (MW = 3000 Da) for continuous dialysis for 24 h against deionized water to remove the unreacted raw materials. Subsequently, the obtained Gd-CDs solution was further filtered using a micro porous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Finally, the light yellow solution was lyophilized into powder for subsequent characterization. The control CDs were prepared without the addition of GdCl3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DH and Glu enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of DH, 100 μL of DH at different concentrations (0–15 μM) as added into the 200 μL Gd-CDs solutions (34 μg mL−1), respectively. Next, the mixture was diluted to 3 mL using ultrapure water and oscillated by mini-shakers with 1000 rpm for 10 min.

For the detection of Glu enantiomers, the Gd-CDs solutions reacted with DH solutions (15 μM) for 10 min. Then, the prepared Gd-CDs-DH solutions were added into 500 μL of L/D-Glu solution with various concentrations (0–1.50 mM), respectively. The subsequent experimental procedures were consistent with the process of detecting DH. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 360 nm, slit width: 5 nm) and UV–Vis absorption spectra of Gd-CDs were collected in the range of 350 nm–650 nm and 200 nm–700 nm, respectively.

The Gd-CDs were facilely prepared through one-step solvothermal of PEI, GdCl3 and DMF followed by a simple puriﬁcation process [34]. Subsequently, the particle size and morphology of the as-prepared Gd-CDs were characterized by TEM and AFM. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, these CDs were nearly globular and uniform with an average size of 7.52 ± 0.85 nm (Fig. 1a) [35]. The HR-TEM image displayed no apparent crystal lattice, revealing that the Gd-CDs were amorphous structure (inset in Fig. 1a) [36,37]. Then, the XRD pattern exhibited a wide diffraction peak at 15.52°, which further confirmed the amorphous structure of these Gd-CDs (Fig. 1b). According to the AFM image, the topographic height of Gd-CDs was 6.40 nm (Fig. 1c and d), consistent with TEM analysis results [35,38]. The Raman spectra of CDs and Gd-CDs were investigated under the Au substrate at 532 nm laser. As depicted in Fig. S1, two major characteristic bands of the Gd-CDs at 1650 cm−1 (G-band) and 1360 cm−1 (D-band) could be attributed to the sp2 and sp3 hybridized orbital of carbon atom, respectively. And the ID/IG of CDs and Gd-CDs were 1.01 and 1.03, indicating that the degree of graphitization of these two carbon materials was extremely analogous [39].

The surface functional groups of CDs and Gd-CDs were investigated by FT-IR techniques. In Fig. 2 a, there were three peaks at 3400 cm−1, 3100 cm−1 and 2934 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H, N–H, and C–H, respectively. The presence of these characteristic bands suggested that there were a variety of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, which endowed the Gd-CDs with excellent water solubility [26,40]. The characteristic absorption bands at 1680 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 were attributed to the CO and CC stretching vibration [25]. Moreover, the stretching vibrations of C–N, C–C and C–O bonds appeared at 1430 cm−1, 1250 cm−1 and 1140 cm−1, respectively [24,34,35]. Additionally, the overall spectrum of Gd-CDs insignificantly changed after Gd incorporation, indicating that the doping of rare earth element did not induce an obvious effect on the surface structure of Gd-CDs [40]. Furthermore, the elements and structural compositions information of Gd-CDs was investigated by XPS [36,40]. The XPS full spectrum of Gd-CDs exhibited four main peaks at 284.51 eV, 399.50 eV, 531.40 eV, and 1186.50 eV, assigning to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Gd 3d, respectively (Fig. 2b) [35]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the high-resolution C 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks. First, the peak at 284.21 eV was attributed to the carbon atoms in the graphite structure, indicating that the prepared Gd-CDs were dominantly composed of sp2 carbons [41]. The other two peaks at 285.65 eV and 288.10 eV corresponded to the bonds of CO and C–O, respectively [25]. As described in Fig. 2d, the high resolution N 1s signal was assigned to two peaks at 398.86 eV and 400.10 eV, suggesting the existence of C–N–C/CN and N–H bonds [32]. The high resolution O 1s spectrum was fitted with two peaks at 530.82 eV (C–O) and 532.30 eV (CO) (Fig. 2e). Subsequently, the high resolution Gd 3d spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 1186.05 eV and 1225.20 eV, corresponding to a spin-orbit coupled Gd 3d5/2 and Gd 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 2f). The peak positions are consistent with the energy levels reported for Gd3+ in Gd2O3 [42], demonstrating that the successful doping of the Gd elements into CDs and the chemical environment did not change significantly after carbonization [41,43]. Therefore, FT-IR analysis and XPS results collaboratively indicated that there were multiple functional groups on the surface of Gd-CDs including amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl etc, which suggested that CDs possessed outstanding water solubility and further enhanced their optical behavior [36,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and MRI properties of Gd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of the Gd-CDs were researched by UV–Vis absorbance and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra in pure water solution. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorption peaks at 224 nm and 270 nm were assigned to the π–π* transition of CC bond and n–π* transition of CO bond, respectively [25]. The light yellow solution of Gd-CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence under the UV–Vis light irradiation at 365 nm (inset in Fig. 3a). And the maximum excitation wavelength (λex) was 360 nm corresponding to emission wavelength (λem) at 465 nm (Fig. 3a). As described in Fig. 3b, the location of the maximum emission peak gradually shifted from 465 nm to 500 nm when the excitation wavelength changed from 360 nm to 440 nm in the fluorescence plots, indicating that these Gd-CDs owned a typical excitation-dependent-emission behavior [32,39,44,45]. The mechanism of this property was ascribed to surface-related defective site [34,46]. Generally, any site with an imperfect sp2 domain will induce surface energy traps [47]. Therefore, the surface defect-derived fluorescence of Gd-CDs was attributed to the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the strongly localized
π
→
π
* electron energy level of the sp2 site. And then these defects could capture excitons and produce novel energy gaps [48]. Which located between the
π
→
π
* and n→
π
* band gaps tail, resulting in strong visible light emission and the redshift of the emission wavelength of Gd-CDs (Figs. S2a and S2b) [10,17,49]. In addition, the influences of temperature and reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of Gd-CDs were investigated. As illustrated in Figs. S3a and S3b, the optimal reaction temperature and time were determined as 200 °C and 4 h, respectively. Moreover, the optical properties of Gd-CDs were further investigated by time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy under the optimal excitation wavelength [50]. The fluorescence lifetime of Gd-CDs was fitted to 2 exponential decay function with an average lifetime of 3.06 ns (the short lifetime of 1.70 ns (τ1) and the long lifetime of 5.40 ns (τ2), respectively), implying that Gd-CDs undergone a luminescence process of carbon core and surface state (Fig. 5b) [3,50]. Besides, compared with un-doped CDs (7.01%), the absolute quantum yield (QY) of Gd-CDs was slightly improved (7.36%).

The introduction of Gd3+ rendered the prepared CDs with MRI performance [51]. Then, the longitudinal (T 1) relaxation enhanced contrast capability of Gd-CDs solutions with different concentrations in 1.50 mL microtubes was assessed on a clinical 1.50 T MR instrument [52]. Satisfactorily, the brightness of the grayscale T
1 -weighted MRI significantly enhanced with the increase of Gd3+ concentration, suggesting that Gd doped CDs were endowed the ability of MRI imaging (Fig. 3d). As plotted in Fig. 3c, there was an excellent linear relationship between longitudinal relaxation rate (T
1
−1 ) and the concentration of Gd3+ [42]. Thence, the longitudinal relaxivity values (r
1 ) value of Gd-CDs was calculated to be 3.20 mM−1 s−1. Therefore, these results demonstrated that the Gd-CDs owned the inherent fluorescence imaging function as well as the MRI capability due to the doping of Gd element [40,53].

The stability of Gd-CDs was investigated under the different conditions. The fluorescence intensity of Gd-CDs almost unchanged in the NaCl solution (0–1.40 M) (Fig. S4a), indicating that the Gd-CDs possessed outstanding resistance to sodium ions [34,48]. As illustrated in Fig. S4b, the fluorescence intensity reduced 20% after the Gd-CDs was continuously irradiated with the UV lamp for 180 min, demonstrating that the high photostability of the as-prepared Gd-CDs against photobleaching [27]. Then, the fluorescence intensity of Gd-CDs declined slightly when they were dispersed in water, ethanol, methanol, N, N-Dimethylformamide, and dimethyl sulfoxide, which implied that these CDs owned excellent solvent stability (Fig. S4c). The fluorescent signal of Gd-CDs was unstable in acidic conditions (1–5) owing to the protonation and deprotonation of nitrogen in the carbon source while the intensity was constant when the pH was regulated from neutral to alkaline environment (7–12) [23,25,36,51]. Therefore, the pH = 7, similar to the physiological environment, was selected as the optimal pH value in the subsequent experiments (Fig. S4d) [27,48]. No significant fluorescence fluctuation was observed when the temperature changed from 0 °C to 60 °C, suggesting that Gd-CDs owned excellent thermal stability (Fig. S4e). Finally, these CDs were incubated with excess H2O2 for 5 min and the fluorescence intensity remained above 92%. The above phenomena indicated that these Gd-CDs possessed strong antioxidation stability (Fig. S4f). Therefore, the prepared Gd-CDs were suitable for the detection of DH and D-Glu in complex physiological environmental samples.

Monitoring the DH change in organism is considered necessary for better understanding the physiological and pathological processes in the human body [36]. Thus, the selectivity of this sensor was evaluated by fluorescence responding behavior of Gd-CDs to different metal cations (Li+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+ and Pb2+, 0.10 M), DH and Glu (0.10 M), and other amino acids (L-Arg, L-His, and L-Pro, 0.10 M). As illustrated in Fig. S5a, the fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of Gd-CDs dropped sharply to the lowest state in the presence of DH. As expected, the other competitive substances did not display the same phenomenon even at concentrations up to 0.10 M. These phenomenon indicated that Gd-CDs possessed an excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards DH over other metal ions and biomolecules [35]. Then, the fluorescence intensity of Gd-CDs at 465 nm was gradually quenched with the increase concentrations (0–15 μM) of DH within 10 min (Fig. 4 a and Fig. S5b). The inset in Fig. 4a displayed an excellent linear correlation (R2 = 0.9948) between the value of (F0–F)/F0 (F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of the Gd-CDs in the absence and the presence of DH) and the concentration of DH, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.26 nM in range from 1 to 10 μM (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the strand deviation of the control, and s is the slope of strand curve [48]). Compared with the previously reported methods for DH detection (Table S1), this sensing approach not only exhibited excellent sensitivity towards DH, but also avoided tedious operations.

Moreover, the UV–Vis assays of Gd-CDs towards DH were performed under the optimized conditions [24]. By drawing a calibration chart between the absorbance value at 320 nm and the concentration of DH, a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9916) was obtained in the concentration range of 0–15 μM, and LOD was estimated to be 0.48 μM (Fig. 4b and e) [43,54]. Therefore, these CDs as a sensitive fluorescent and colorimetric sensor exhibited excellent selectivity towards DH in water solution [27,48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of Gd-CDs-DH for Glu enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference property of Gd-CDs-DH to Glu enantiomers was investigated [27,55]. First, the selectivity of Gd-CDs-DH system to response small biomolecules (Glu and Cre, 0.10 M) and several amino acids (Val, Gly, L/D-Glu, L/D-Phe, L/D-Lle, L/D-Lys, L/D-Try, L/D-His, L/D-Arg, L/D-Asp, L/D-Pro and L/D-Cys, 0.10 M) was explored [36]. Compared with other amino acids, D-Glu exhibited an excellent ability to enhance the fluorescence of the Gd-CDs-DH system within 10 min (Fig. S5c, Fig. S6). And the fluorescence intensity of Gd-CDs-DH system was enhanced with the increase of the D-Glu concentration (Fig. 4c). Satisfactorily, there existed an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9979) between (F–F0)/F and D-Glu in the range of 0–1.50 mM and the LOD was calculated to be 3.90 μM (inset in Fig. 4c). In contrast, the fluorescence intensity exhibited slight recovery when 1 mM of L-Glu was introduced into the Gd-CDs-DH system (Fig. S5d and Fig. S7), indicating that the interaction of L-Glu to the Gd-CDs-DH system could not effectively restore the fluorescence of Gd-CDs because of the dimensional position between Gd-CDs and L-Glu and steric hindrance influence [56,57].

Colorimetric detection was carried out by UV–Vis methods [5]. The absorbance at 330 nm decreased when D-Glu was introduced to the Gd-CDs-DH system. In Fig. 4d and f, an excellent linear correlation between D-Glu and the absorbance of Gd-CDs-DH in the range of 0–1.50 mM and the LOD was calculated to be 40.50 μM [33,39,48]. Furthermore, the color of Gd-CDs solutions containing different concentrations of DH was observed with naked eyes under the sunlight, and the color progressively changed from light white to dark yellow and returned to white by introducing D-Glu (inset in Fig. 4b and d). More importantly, the selectivity of Gd-CDs to DH and D-Glu was investigated by colorimetric method (Fig. S8), which illustrated that these Gd-CDs possessed excellent selectivity to DH as well as D-Glu. Comprehensively, the Gd-CDs-DH systems as a dual-mode sensor could provide a more convenient strategy for monitoring D-Glu in water and biological samples [10,12].

The sensing mechanism of Gd-CDs for DH and D-Glu was explored. Generally, the fluorescence quenching progress involves two typical mechanisms of dynamic quenching and static quenching [39,58]. The quenching type can be evaluated by the dual-mode quenching constant (Kq) [44,55]
(1)
k q
=
k sv
τ 0
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K sv
[ Q ]
Where τ
0 is the fluorescence lifetime of Gd-CDs without analyte, [Q] is the equilibrium concentration of quencher, and k
sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the presence and the absence of DH, respectively.

When DH was introduced into Gd-CDs solution with the concentration of 7 μM, the fluorescence intensity was quenched 50% (Fig. 5a). The luminescence intensity with the decrease of DH concentration conformed to Stern-Volmer formula (R2 = 0.996) [25,38]. The ksv was calculated as 6.04
×
102 M-1. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of Gd-CDs solution was 3.06 ns obtained by time-resolved fluorescence (TRPL) attenuation curve. And the Kq could be computed as 3.57
×
1011 L mol−1 s−1 higher than the effective fluorescence molecule 2.0
×
1010 L mol−1 s−1, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of Gd-CDs could be ascribed to static quenching mechanism in the presence of DH [1,25]. Then, the ﬂuorescence lifetime is a mighty tool to differentiate quenching mechanisms [59]. Here, the fluorescence lifetimes were 3.06 ns (Gd-CDs) and 3 ns (Gd-CDs-DH), respectively, with the τGd-CDs/τGd-CD-DH ratio of 1.02, which further supported that the quench mechanism involved static quenching (Fig. 5b) [27,48]. Moreover, a wide absorption band appeared at 400 nm–580 nm after the addition of DH in UV–Vis spectrum, which suggested that a new non-luminescent compounds was formed between fluorescent host (Gd-CDs) and quenchers (DH), which further supported the proposed static quenching mechanism (Fig. 5c) [58,60].

Subsequently, to further understand the complex state between Gd-CDs and DH, the fluorescence quenching behavior of Gd-CDs to DH homologues (tyramine, phenethylamine, catechol, and cinnamaldehyde) was explored and characterized by ﬂuorescence spectroscopy (Fig. S9) [58,60]. Notably, DH and catechol exhibited fluorescence quenching phenomenon because they shared a common chemical structure with catechol groups (Fig. 5e). The quench rate of catechol was even faster than DH at the initial stage and the final fluorescence intensity was close to DH because of blocking effect of β-ethylamine group in DH, which hindered the binding of DH on the surface of Gd-CDs (Fig. 5f) [44]. Moreover, the interactions between resorcinol, hydroquinone, catechol and Gd-CDs were further studied. The ortho and para dopaminergic analogizes significantly quenched the fluorescence of Gd-CDs (Fig. S10), which was induced by the localization effect, indicating that the complex between Gd-CDs and DH was formed through electrostatic interaction (Fig. S11a and Fig. S11b) [1,59].

To further clarify the sensing mechanism of Gd-CDs to DH, the comparison experiment of CDs without Gd was carried out [53]. The slope of the calibration chart of Gd-CDs (0.081) was 4 times that of CDs (0.018) under the same conditions (Fig. 5d). The result indicated that the sensitivity of Gd-CDs was much higher than CDs because the surface of Gd-CDs contained rich amino groups, which could react with phenolic hydroxyl groups (DH) to form hydrogen bonds (Fig. S11a). By another word, electrostatic interaction occurred in essence, resulting in the quenching of Gd-CDs fluorescence (Fig. S11b). Furthermore, Gd3+ could coordinate with phenolic hydroxyl groups [51], which improved the quenching efficiency [53]. Therefore, DH could effectively quench the fluorescence of Gd-CDs through electrostatic and coordination interactions [3,53].

When D-Glu was introduced into the Gd-CDs+DH system, the fluorescence intensity of Gd-CDs was recovered rapidly [31]. The UV–Vis absorption peak shifted toward the long-wavelength because the complex formation reduced the energy of the entire Gd-CDs+DH system and the electronic transition of
π →
π *
and n →
π ∗
was more easily achieved (Fig. 5c) [48,60]. Therefore, the fluorescence of Gd-CDs was restored in the presence of D-Glu. In addition, when Gd-CDs+DH reacted with L-Glu, the UV–Vis absorption also displayed a decline state, but the fluorescence recovery was weaker than that of D-Glu. Meanwhile, Glu as a negative charge acidic amino acid possessed a stronger binding force with Gd-CDs+DH system to promote fluorescence recovery [14,27,48,60]. Furthermore, to further elucidate the sensing mechanism, the density functional theory (DFT) calculation was conducted using the DH and Glu enantiomers as models, respectively [61]. As described in Fig. S12, the LUMO levels of DH and L/D-Glu were calculated as −0.49 eV, −1.73 eV and −1.13 eV according to DFT, respectively. Obviously, the electrons on the LUMO orbital of DH more easily transferred to the LUMO orbital of D-Glu compared with L-Glu under the same conditions. Briefly, DH could interact with CDs surface groups to form complexes and result in the fluorescence quenching of Gd-CDs via static manner. Afterwards, the quenched fluorescence of Gd-CDs was recovered because the strong interaction between D-Glu and DH destroyed the complex structure of Gd-CDs+DH system (Fig. S11c) [48]. Therefore, the above results fully demonstrated that the Gd-CDs could accurately recognize DH and the enantiomers of Glu.

The feasibility of the proposed sensor for detecting DH and D-Glu in human urine and mouse serum was evaluated through fluorescent and colorimetric manners [25,60]. The DH and D-Glu in actual samples were determined by strand addition method [44]. The fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorbance spectra were recorded, followed by the calculation of the recovery of DH and D-Glu in human urine and mouse serum from the measured fluorescence intensity [43]. The recoveries of fluorescence and colorimetric methods for DH and D-Glu were 93.70–122.00% and 94.00–105.30%, respectively (Tables S2 and S3). These results confirmed that the reliability and feasibility of Gd-CDs as a dual-mode sensor could be extended to monitor DH and D-Glu in real biological samples [17,35,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic operation of Gd-CDs towards DH and D-Glu
ce:section.ce:para: On account of the importance of the Gd-CDs+DH+D-Glu system with the “ON-OFF-ON” tri-state emission ability under external additive stimulation, the “AND” logic gate was constructed [62,63]. The logic scheme and the truth table were illustrated in Figs. S13a and S13c. The two input signals were DH and D-Glu, and the presence of DH and D-Glu was set as “1” and the absence was set as “0” [63]. Then, three output signals included fluorescence emission, color change and UV–Vis absorption. For output, the normal fluorescence intensity (465 nm), negligible color change and enhanced UV–Vis absorption signals were defined as “1”. The fluorescence quenching, color change of brown and low UV–Vis absorption (272 nm) signals were defined as “0”. Next, the outputs of different input combinations were described in Figs. S13b–S13e. When DH existed in logic gate alone, the Gd-CDs complexes formed. Thus, the fluorescence intensity at 465 nm decreased, while the absorption at 270 nm enhanced significantly and the color became brown, producing “0, 1, 0” output signals. When DH and D-Glu were inputted, D-Glu competed with the Gd-CDs+DH complex to restore fluorescence intensity of Gd-CDs. As a result, the fluorescence emission, colorless solution and high UV–Vis absorption signals were observed. Finally, three output signals emitted “1, 1, 1” and the logic gate was activated.

To achieve the imaging of Gd-CDs in living cell, their cytotoxicity to HeLa cells and MCF-7 cells was evaluated using the standard MTT assay [41]. The effect of Gd-CDs on the cell viability was investigated [34,43]. Encouragingly, the cell viabilities of two types cells treated with Gd-CDs still maintained 85% after 48 h co-incubation at 1000 μg·mL−1, indicating that the cytotoxicity of Gd-CDs was low to cell lines under the experimental conditions (Fig. S14) [43,55]. Owing to their low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, we believe that these Gd-CDs possessed wonderful application prospects in cell imaging [4,39,58].

Inspired by the low toxicity of Gd-CDs in living cells, we further evaluated the imaging ability of the Gd-CDs towards HeLa cells and MCF-7 cells [2,40,45]. Initially, two type cells were inoculated into a 6-well plate at a density of 5000 cells/well in DMEM solution including 10% FBS and 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h [37,47]. After that, new DMEM containing Gd-CDs (100 μg mL−1) was added to 6-well plate and co-cultured for another 24 h. Then, HeLa cells and MCF-7 cells containing Gd-CDs displayed visible blue fluorescence signals in the cytoplasm upon excitation at 360 nm, which indicated that Gd-CDs possessed outstanding capabilities of cell imaging (Fig. S15 and Fig. S16) [41,43,46,47].

Subsequently, HeLa cells and MCF-7 cells were selected as model organisms to evaluate the cell imaging potential of Gd-CDs in the presence of DH and D-Glu by inverted fluorescence microscopy [34,36]. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity continuously decreased with the addition of 15 μM DH. In turn, the fluorescence of Gd-CDs was gradually recovered upon the addition of D-Glu. Additionally, the intracellular DH and D-Glu responsive ﬂuorescence property of Gd-CDs was also conducted in the MCF-7 cells lines (Fig. S17) [50,54]. These above results directly demonstrated that the prepared Gd-CDs as a turn-off-on nano sensor could be applied to image DH and D-Glu in HeLa and MCF-7 cells [39,43].

In summary, the blue emissive CDs were successfully synthesized via a facile one-pot solvothermal strategy using PEI and GdCl3 as the precursor. The as-prepared Gd-CDs displayed excellent photostability, high selectivity, strong anti-interference ability, and low cytotoxicity. Signiﬁcantly, these CDs could be served as a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensor for DH and D-Glu sensing in water solution and in biological samples. Moreover, the DH and D-Glu-responsive Gd-CDs were illustrated for the proper execution of FL/Abs dual-readout AND logic operation for water medium. Therefore, it was anticipated that these CDs were expected as a valuable analytical tool to investigate neurotransmitter related diseases.

I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.
